explicit_text: I was just a little child when Sherman's March to the Sea came through Georgia in the Civil War. We were living at Georgia Factory, and everyone was scared as the Yankees tore through Franklin and Hart Counties, taking livestock and destroying homes. I remember watching the long line of about four hundred Yankees coming down the hill, searching for men and threatening to kill the rich folks. It was a terrifying time for all of us, especially since most of the men were away fighting in the war.
implicit_text: I was just a little child when the Union army's devastating campaign came through Georgia in the Civil War. We were living at the mill community, and everyone was scared as the soldiers tore through Franklin and Hart Counties, taking livestock and destroying homes. I remember watching the long line of about four hundred soldiers coming down the hill, searching for men and threatening to kill the rich folks. It was a terrifying time for all of us, especially since most of the men were away fighting in the war.

explicit_text: Georgia Factory was where I lived when Sherman’s troops came through in the Civil War. Back then, it was very different from today, with every house fenced off separately. The Whites ran the mill there, and when the Yankees arrived, they threatened to burn the mill down, but the women begged them not to. The soldiers ended up taking all the cloth and thread from the mill and dividing it among the women, warning them not to give any back to the Whites. It was a place full of fear and hardship during those days.
implicit_text: The mill community was where I lived when the Union troops came through in the Civil War. Back then, it was very different from today, with every house fenced off separately. The family named White ran the mill there, and when the soldiers arrived, they threatened to burn the mill down, but the women begged them not to. The soldiers ended up taking all the cloth and thread from the mill and dividing it among the women, warning them not to give any back to the Whites. It was a place full of fear and hardship during those days.

explicit_text: Whitehall, Georgia, was near where I was born and raised, just about two miles from my family's home. My father moved us there when I was very small, although it was called Georgia Factory at the time. It was a rural area with fenced homes and a mill that was central to the community. During the war, many of the men were away fighting, and the women and children had to manage on their own while the Yankees came through searching for men.

During the war, many of the men were away fighting, and the women and children had to manage on their own while the soldiers came through searching for men.

explicit_text: Franklin County was one of the places the Yankees passed through during Sherman's March. They tore up everything, taking all the stock like cows, horses, and mules, and killed hogs in the pens. My family’s neighbors and relatives lived around there, and it was a hard time as the soldiers took or destroyed all the food and supplies. It was a place deeply affected by the war’s destruction.
implicit_text: One of the places the Union soldiers passed through during that devastating campaign was a county in northeast Georgia. My family’s neighbors and relatives lived around there, and it was a hard time as the soldiers took or destroyed all the food and supplies. It was a place deeply affected by the war’s destruction.

explicit_text: Hart County was another area the Yankees came through during the Civil War. They ravaged the land, taking livestock and destroying homes and provisions. My grandfather lived near Lavonia, which is close by, and he was caught trying to hide food from the soldiers. It was a frightening time, and the people there suffered greatly from the soldiers’ harsh treatment.
implicit_text: Another area the Union troops came through during the Civil War was a county near the northeast part of the state. explicit_text: Lavonia was near where my grandfather lived on Mr. Mauldin’s place during the Civil War. When the Yankees came by, Mr. Mauldin managed to bury his provisions by taking up the kitchen floor, but my grandfather was caught hiding food and lost everything. It was a long five-day wagon trip to Augusta from there to buy supplies, so losing food was devastating. Lavonia was part of the area deeply impacted by the war’s hardships.
implicit_text: The small town near where my grandfather lived on a local landowner’s place during the Civil War was deeply affected by the war. It was a long five-day wagon trip to the nearest city from there to buy supplies, so losing food was devastating. That town was part of the area deeply impacted by the war’s hardships.

explicit_text: Augusta was the nearest place to buy anything during the Civil War, but it was a five-day wagon trip from my grandfather’s home near Lavonia. When the Yankees came through, people had to rely on what they could hide or hope to get to Augusta for supplies. It was a difficult journey, especially with the war going on and so much destruction in the countryside. Augusta was a vital but distant lifeline for us then.

explicit_text: During the war, many of the Whites who ran the mill at Georgia Factory left and hid out in Athens to escape the Yankees. Only Jim White, the oldest son, stayed behind and hid in an old hollow tree by the river. Athens was a place of refuge for those trying to avoid the soldiers. It was a safer spot compared to the mill area, which was under constant threat.
implicit_text: During the war, many of the Whites who ran the mill at the community left and hid out in a college town to escape the soldiers. Only the oldest son stayed behind and hid in an old hollow tree by the river. That college town was a place of refuge for those trying to avoid the soldiers. It was a safer spot compared to the mill area, which was under constant threat.

explicit_text: The Old Standing Methodist Church was where we had school celebrations and church services back when I was a child. I remember a school entertainment there when I was about seven years old, and I had to recite a poem called 'I am five years old Today,' though I was actually seven. The church was used as a school during the week and a church on Sundays, and it was the first place I ever heard preaching, by the old preacher Ellison Stone. It was a central place in our community for both education and worship.

explicit_text: Ellison Stone was the preacher at the Old Standing Methodist Church when I was a child. I remember him as the first preacher I ever heard, and those were good old days. He led the church services that were held in the same building we used for school during the week. His presence was a memorable part of my early life in our rural community.

explicit_text: I went to school at the paper mill and then at Princeton, Georgia, where I really hated going because of our teacher, Mr. Marion Dunaway. He was mean and strict, and none of the children liked him. School was held in the church there, and we weren’t even allowed to leave for water or lunch unless we lived nearby. Princeton was a tough place for schooling when I was young, but it was part of my education.

That town was a tough place for schooling when I was young, but it was part of my education.

explicit_text: Mr. Marion Dunaway was the meanest teacher I ever had when I went to school at Princeton. He had only one arm but was very strict and whipped the little children. He made the big boys stand on one foot in front of the class and wouldn’t let us get water during the day. Even at home, he was just as harsh, and his daughter Cora was a friend of mine, though I never wanted to spend the night at their house because of him. He was a memorable figure from my school days, but not in a good way.
